claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 15 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 15 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to understand your situation. 

**First, let me ask about the child (or children) you're claiming for:**

Could you please tell me:
1. **How many children** are you looking to claim Child Benefit for?
2. **What is the name and age** of each child?

This will help me determine which eligibility pathway applies to your situation. Child Benefit eligibility depends significantly on whether the child is:
- Under 16 years old
- Between 16-19 years old (with specific education requirements)
- 20 years or older

What are the details of the child or children you're asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes, I'm calling about my son Alex. He's 15 years old and I wanted to check if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me help you determine eligibility for Child Benefit for your son Alex who is 15 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, he falls into the automatic qualification age category. Now I need to check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since he's 15, this is typically straightforward - most 15-year-olds either don't work or work part-time hours. Can you confirm whether Alex:
- Is not working at all, OR
- Works less than 24 hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all. He's still at school full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's helpful. Since Alex doesn't work and is still in full-time school, he meets this requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now the next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, I need to confirm that Alex is not receiving:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA), OR
- Universal Credit

...in his own name. Since he's 15 and in full-time school, this typically doesn't apply, but I need to confirm. Does Alex receive any benefits like ESA or Universal Credit in his own right?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just a regular school kid.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's another requirement met. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about apprenticeships:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program?**

Since you mentioned he's in regular school, I'm assuming he's not in an apprenticeship, but I need to confirm. Is Alex:
- NOT in any apprenticeship program at all, OR
- In a Foundation Apprenticeship in Wales (which is approved)?

Most 15-year-olds in full-time school are not doing apprenticeships, so this should be straightforward.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not doing an apprenticeship. He's just doing his regular GCSEs at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex is in regular school doing his GCSEs, so he's not in any apprenticeship. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about your residency status. This is an important requirement for claiming Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (most common), OR
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
3. You have pre-settled status and meet certain conditions (working, studying, or have sufficient resources), OR
4. You're a Crown servant living abroad

For most people, the first option applies. **Do you normally live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You meet the residency requirement as you normally live in the UK. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex** (most common), OR
2. You contribute to his upkeep (paying at least the Child Benefit amount for things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing), OR
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

**Do you live with Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You meet the responsibility requirement as Alex lives with you. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. For example:
- Is Alex's other parent claiming Child Benefit for him?
- Is anyone else (like a grandparent or other guardian) claiming for him?

If someone else is claiming but you've agreed that you should claim instead, that's fine - you can transfer the claim. 

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! No one else is claiming for Alex, so there's no conflict. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Alex's living situation to see if any special rules apply.

**Which of the following best describes Alex's current living situation?**

1. **Living normally at home with you** (most common)
2. Currently in hospital for a prolonged period
3. In local authority care
4. In a fostering arrangement

Since you mentioned Alex lives at home with you and is at school, I'm assuming it's option 1 - living normally at home. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. He's just living at home with me, nothing unusual.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is living normally at home with you. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Just one final check:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the local council paying for Alex's living costs or upkeep? Since Alex lives at home with you and is just a regular school student, I expect the answer is that the council is NOT paying for him. Can you confirm that?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct, the council isn't paying anything for him. I cover all his costs myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Based on all the information you've provided, let me navigate to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

Based on all the information you've provided, **you meet all the eligibility requirements** to claim Child Benefit for your son Alex.

### Why You're Eligible:

✅ **Alex is a qualifying child** - He's 15 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in his own name, and not in an apprenticeship

✅ **You meet the claimant requirements** - You live in the UK, you're responsible for Alex (living together), no one else is claiming for him, and the local council isn't paying for his care

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online** or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay back some or all of the benefit through the tax system (though you can still claim)
5. **Payments**: Child Benefit is paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### More Information:

You can apply and find more details here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 15 years old, which is under 16 and automatically qualifies in terms of age; (2) He is not working (still in full-time school doing GCSEs), meeting the requirement of working less than 24 hours per week; (3) He does not receive any benefits in his own right such as ESA or Universal Credit; (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship - he's in regular school education; (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex lives normally at home, not in hospital, care, or fostering; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for their 15-year-old son Alex. Alex meets all the qualifying child criteria (age under 16, not working full-time, not receiving benefits, not in disqualifying apprenticeship), and the claimant meets all the claimant criteria (UK residency, responsibility for the child, no other claimant, no local authority financial support).

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 15 years old, which is under 16 and automatically qualifies in terms of age; (2) He is not working (still in full-time school doing GCSEs), meeting the requirement of working less than 24 hours per week; (3) He does not receive any benefits in his own right such as ESA or Universal Credit; (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship - he's in regular school education; (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex lives normally at home, not in hospital, care, or fostering; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for their 15-year-old son Alex. Alex meets all the qualifying child criteria (age under 16, not working full-time, not receiving benefits, not in disqualifying apprenticeship), and the claimant meets all the claimant criteria (UK residency, responsibility for the child, no other claimant, no local authority financial support).
